title: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances
section: Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function

items.title: Total-body water is split between the ICF and ECF, and the ECF further between plasma and interstitium
items.content:
Water is the most abundant body constituent, comprising roughly half of body weight in women and 60% in men.
Total-body water is distributed between two major compartments: 55-75% is intracellular (ICF) and 25-45% is extracellular (ECF).
The ECF itself is subdivided into the intravascular (plasma water) and extravascular (interstitial) spaces in a ratio of about 1:3, with fluid movement between them governed by Starling forces across the capillary wall.
keyPoints: Total-body water is ~50% of body weight in women and ~60% in men
ICF accounts for 55-75% and ECF for 25-45% of total-body water
The ECF is split roughly 1:3 between intravascular (plasma) and interstitial spaces
items.reference: COMPOSITION OF BODY FLUIDS: "Water is the most abundant constituent in the body, comprising ~50% of body weight in women and 60% in men. Total-body water is distributed in two major compartments: 55–75% is intracellular (intracellular fluid [ICF]), and 25–45% is extracellular (extracellular fluid [ECF]). The ECF is further subdivided into intravascular (plasma water) and extravascular (interstitial) spaces in a ratio of 1:3."

items.title: Why AVP release has both a fixed osmotic threshold and a volume-dependent sensitivity
items.content:
AVP secretion and thirst are both triggered once plasma osmolality rises above a threshold of about 285 mOsm/kg, beyond which AVP rises linearly with osmolality.
This osmotic threshold is not fixed, however: ECF volume status shifts both the threshold and the steepness (slope) of the AVP response.
Hypovolemia lowers the osmotic threshold and steepens the slope, so AVP rises earlier and more aggressively for a given rise in osmolality — the body prioritizes restoring volume even at the cost of a lower serum sodium. Hypervolemia does the opposite, raising the threshold and flattening the slope so that AVP (and thus water retention) is suppressed until osmolality is higher.
This volume-osmolality interaction explains why volume-depleted or edematous states (heart failure, cirrhosis) so commonly produce hyponatremia despite normal or even low effective osmolality.
keyPoints: The osmotic threshold for AVP release and thirst is ~285 mOsm/kg, above which AVP rises linearly with osmolality
Hypovolemia lowers the osmotic threshold and increases the AVP response slope — AVP rises 'too easily'
Hypervolemia raises the threshold and flattens the slope, suppressing AVP until osmolality is higher
This volume-osmolality crosstalk is why hypovolemic and hypervolemic states both predispose to hyponatremia
items.reference: COMPOSITION OF BODY FLUIDS: "AVP secretion is stimulated as systemic osmolality increases above a threshold level of ~285 mOsm/kg, above which there is a linear relationship between osmolality and circulating AVP... ECF volume strongly modulates the relationship between circulating osmolality and AVP release, such that hypovolemia reduces the osmotic threshold and increases the slope of the response curve to osmolality; hypervolemia has an opposite effect, increasing the osmotic threshold and reducing the slope of the response curve."

items.title: How AVP-driven aquaporin-2 insertion sets urine osmolality between 30-50 and up to 1200 mOsm/kg
items.content:
AVP acting on renal V2 receptors triggers PKA-dependent phosphorylation of the aquaporin-2 (AQP2) water channel in principal cells of the collecting duct, causing AQP2 to be inserted into the apical (luminal) membrane.
Once inserted, AQP2 allows water to move passively down the medullary osmotic gradient established by the countercurrent mechanism, so the collecting-duct fluid equilibrates with the hypertonic medullary interstitium — producing concentrated urine with an osmolality of up to 1200 mOsm/kg under maximal antidiuresis.
When circulating AVP is absent, AQP2 insertion is essentially abolished, the collecting duct stays water-impermeable, and a dilute urine as low as 30-50 mOsm/kg is excreted.
This 'final common pathway' — AVP-to-V2-to-AQP2 — is the step disrupted in most water-balance disorders, whether the defect is reduced AVP (central DI), reduced AQP2 insertion (nephrogenic DI), or inappropriately persistent AVP/AQP2 activity (SIAD).
keyPoints: AVP-V2-PKA signaling phosphorylates AQP2 and drives its insertion into the collecting-duct apical membrane
With AQP2 inserted, urine equilibrates with the hypertonic medullary interstitium — up to 1200 mOsm/kg
Without AVP, AQP2 insertion is abolished and urine osmolality can fall to 30-50 mOsm/kg
This AVP-AQP2 axis is the 'final common pathway' disrupted in central DI, nephrogenic DI, and SIAD
items.reference: SODIUM AND WATER: "AVP-induced, PKA-dependent phosphorylation of the aquaporin-2 water channel in principal cells stimulates the insertion of active water channels into the lumen of the CD, resulting in transepithelial water absorption down the medullary osmotic gradient... Under \"antidiuretic\" conditions, with increased circulating AVP, the kidney reabsorbs water filtered by the glomerulus, equilibrating the osmolality across the CD epithelium to excrete a hypertonic, \"concentrated\" urine (osmolarity of up to 1200 mOsm/kg). In the absence of circulating AVP, insertion of aquaporin-2 channels and water absorption across the CD is essentially abolished, resulting in secretion of a hypotonic, dilute urine (osmolarity as low as 30–50 mOsm/kg)."

items.title: Renal and extrarenal mechanisms that produce true volume depletion
items.content:
Hypovolemia (true volume depletion) reflects combined salt and water loss that contracts the ECF volume, and its causes are divided into renal and nonrenal categories.
Renal causes include osmotic diuresis from a high filtered solute load (glucose, urea, mannitol), pharmacologic diuretics, drugs that impair tubular Na+-Cl- reabsorption (acetazolamide, trimethoprim, pentamidine), hereditary tubular transport defects, mineralocorticoid deficiency/resistance, and tubulointerstitial injury.
Extrarenal causes include gastrointestinal losses (vomiting, diarrhea), insensible losses from skin and respiratory tract, and 'third-spacing' of fluid into the interstitium, peritoneum, or bowel lumen in sepsis, burns, pancreatitis, or obstruction.
keyPoints: Renal causes of hypovolemia include osmotic diuresis, diuretics, tubular transport defects, and mineralocorticoid deficiency/resistance
Extrarenal causes include GI losses, insensible (skin/respiratory) losses, and third-spacing into interstitium, peritoneum, or bowel
Tubulointerstitial injury (interstitial nephritis, ATI, obstructive uropathy) can also reduce distal Na+-Cl- and water absorption
items.reference: HYPOVOLEMIA: "RENAL CAUSES Excessive urinary Na⁺-Cl⁻ and water loss is a feature of several conditions. A high filtered load of endogenous solutes, such as glucose and urea, can impair tubular reabsorption of Na⁺-Cl⁻ and water, leading to an osmotic diuresis... EXTRARENAL CAUSES Nonrenal causes of hypovolemia include fluid loss from the gastrointestinal tract, skin, and respiratory system. Accumulations of fluid within specific tissue compartments, typically the interstitium, peritoneum, or gastrointestinal tract, can also cause hypovolemia."

items.title: Why urine Na+ and urine Cl- are interpreted differently in hypovolemia with alkalosis
items.content:
In nonrenal (extrarenal) hypovolemia, the neurohumoral response drives renal Na+ and water retention, so urine Na+ is typically <20 mM and urine osmolality is >450 mOsm/kg.
However, patients who are hypovolemic from vomiting, diarrhea, or diuretics often have a hypochloremic alkalosis with increased filtered HCO3-. The bicarbonaturia obligates Na+ excretion (as the accompanying cation), so urine Na+ can be >20 mM and urine pH >7.0 even though the patient is volume-depleted.
In this setting, urine Cl- is the more reliable index of volume status — a urine Cl- <25 mM still indicates hypovolemia, because Cl- excretion (unlike Na+) is not being driven by bicarbonaturia.
By contrast, renal causes of hypovolemia (e.g., acute tubular necrosis, DI) typically show an inappropriately high urine Na+ (>20 mM) with a dilute urine, reflecting the primary renal defect rather than an appropriate retention response.
keyPoints: Nonrenal hypovolemia: urine Na+ <20 mM, urine osmolality >450 mOsm/kg (appropriate retention)
With hypochloremic alkalosis (vomiting/diarrhea/diuretics), bicarbonaturia raises urine Na+ and pH despite hypovolemia
Urine Cl- <25 mM remains a reliable marker of hypovolemia in this bicarbonaturic setting
Renal causes of hypovolemia show inappropriately high urine Na+ (>20 mM) with dilute urine
items.reference: HYPOVOLEMIA: "the urine Na⁺ concentration is typically <20 mM in nonrenal causes of hypovolemia, with a urine osmolality of >450 mOsm/kg... patients with hypovolemia and a hypochloremic alkalosis due to vomiting, diarrhea, or diuretics will typically have a urine Na⁺ concentration >20 mM and urine pH of >7.0, due to the increase in filtered HCO₃⁻; the urine Cl⁻ concentration in this setting is a more accurate indicator of volume status, with a level <25 mM suggestive of hypovolemia."

items.title: How the choice of resuscitation fluid is tailored to serum sodium and acid-base status
items.content:
Mild hypovolemia is managed with oral rehydration and resumption of a normal diet, but severe hypovolemia requires intravenous fluids chosen to match the underlying pathophysiology.
Isotonic ('normal') 0.9% saline (154 mM Na+) is the preferred resuscitation fluid for normonatremic or hyponatremic patients with severe hypovolemia; colloids such as IV albumin offer no demonstrated advantage for this purpose.
Hypernatremic patients, by contrast, need a hypotonic fluid: 5% dextrose if the deficit is pure water loss (as in DI), or hypotonic (1/2 or 1/4 normal) saline if both water and Na+-Cl- have been lost — with the rate of free-water administration guided by frequent serum chemistries.
Patients with bicarbonate loss and metabolic acidosis (e.g., diarrhea) should receive intravenous bicarbonate, either as an isotonic solution (150 meq Na-HCO3 in 5% dextrose) or a more hypotonic bicarbonate-containing solution. Patients with severe hemorrhage or anemia should receive red cell transfusion, without raising hematocrit beyond 35%.
keyPoints: Normal (0.9%) saline is preferred for normonatremic/hyponatremic patients with severe hypovolemia
Hypernatremic hypovolemic patients need hypotonic fluids — 5% dextrose for pure water loss, or 1/2-1/4 normal saline for combined water and Na+-Cl- loss
Diarrhea-associated bicarbonate loss and acidosis should be treated with isotonic or hypotonic bicarbonate-containing fluid
Red cell transfusion for severe hemorrhage/anemia should not raise hematocrit beyond 35%
items.reference: TREATMENT Hypovolemia: "Isotonic, \"normal\" saline (0.9% NaCl, 154 mM Na⁺) is the most appropriate resuscitation fluid for normonatremic or hyponatremic patients with severe hypovolemia; colloid solutions such as intravenous albumin are not demonstrably superior for this purpose. Hypernatremic patients should receive a hypotonic solution, 5% dextrose if there has only been water loss (as in DI), or hypotonic saline (1/2 or 1/4 normal saline) if there has been water and Na⁺-Cl⁻ loss... Patients with severe hemorrhage or anemia should receive red cell transfusions, without increasing the hematocrit beyond 35%."

items.title: Hyponatremia is classified by volume status into hypovolemic, euvolemic, and hypervolemic categories
items.content:
Hyponatremia is defined as a plasma Na+ concentration <135 mM and is very common, occurring in up to 22% of hospitalized patients.
Because the underlying pathophysiology of the 'inappropriate' AVP response differs by volume status, hyponatremia is diagnostically subdivided into three groups based on clinical history and examination: hypovolemic, euvolemic, and hypervolemic.
This three-way framework is the backbone of the diagnostic approach to hyponatremia, since each category points to a different set of causes and a different initial therapy.
keyPoints: Hyponatremia = plasma Na+ <135 mM, present in up to 22% of hospitalized patients
Diagnostic classification is by ECF volume status: hypovolemic, euvolemic, or hypervolemic hyponatremia
Each volume category reflects a distinct underlying pattern of AVP dysregulation
items.reference: HYPONATREMIA: "Hyponatremia, which is defined as a plasma Na⁺ concentration <135 mM, is a very common disorder, occurring in up to 22% of hospitalized patients... Hyponatremia is thus subdivided diagnostically into three groups, depending on clinical history and volume status, i.e., \"hypovolemic,\" \"euvolemic,\" and \"hyper-volemic\" (Fig. 56-5)."

items.title: Why nearly all hyponatremia (except low solute intake) reflects an AVP-driven water-retention problem
items.content:
Despite the very different clinical settings, almost all hyponatremia results from the combination of an increase in circulating AVP (and/or increased renal sensitivity to AVP) together with ongoing free-water intake.
In hypovolemic hyponatremia, true volume depletion drives nonosmotic AVP release. In hypervolemic hyponatremia (heart failure, cirrhosis), 'arterial underfilling' triggers the same neurohumoral activation and AVP release despite total-body Na+ and water excess. In most euvolemic hyponatremia (SIAD), AVP is secreted inappropriately for the osmotic/volume state.
The one notable exception is hyponatremia due to low solute intake (e.g., beer potomania), where AVP is appropriately suppressible but the kidney cannot generate enough urine solute to excrete ingested free water.
This unifying concept explains why the cornerstone of treating most hyponatremia is addressing the AVP-water retention axis (fluid restriction, correcting the underlying stimulus, or AVP antagonism) rather than simply 'giving salt.'
keyPoints: Hypovolemic, hypervolemic, and most euvolemic (SIAD) hyponatremia share an AVP-driven water-retention mechanism
Hypervolemic states (CHF, cirrhosis) trigger AVP via 'arterial underfilling' despite total-body Na+/water excess
Low solute intake (beer potomania) is the key exception — AVP is appropriately suppressed, but solute-poor diet limits free-water excretion
items.reference: HYPONATREMIA: "This disorder is almost always the result of an increase in circulating AVP and/or increased renal sensitivity to AVP, combined with an intake of free water; a notable exception is hyponatremia due to low solute intake (see below)."

items.title: How the four recognized patterns of AVP secretion in SIAD inform its diagnosis
items.content:
SIAD (syndrome of inappropriate antidiuresis) is the most frequent cause of euvolemic hyponatremia, and four distinct patterns of AVP secretion have been described, largely independent of the underlying cause.
About one-third of patients have unregulated, erratic AVP secretion with no correlation between serum osmolality and AVP level. Another group fails to suppress AVP at low osmolalities but otherwise has a normal response curve to hyperosmolar stimuli. A third group has a 'reset osmostat' — a left-shifted osmotic threshold and response curve. The fourth group has essentially undetectable circulating AVP, implying either a gain-of-function in renal water reabsorption (e.g., activating V2-receptor mutations causing 'nephrogenic SIAD') or a non-AVP antidiuretic substance.
Recognizing these patterns matters clinically: SIAD generates hyponatremia only when free-water intake continues at osmolalities below the (shifted) thirst threshold, and patients with SIAD are subclinically volume-expanded rather than truly euvolemic, with 'AVP escape' limiting further distal Na+-Cl- and water retention.
keyPoints: ~1/3 of SIAD patients have erratic, unregulated AVP secretion uncorrelated with osmolality
Other patterns: failure to suppress AVP at low osmolality, a 'reset osmostat,' or undetectable AVP (gain-of-function V2 receptor / non-AVP antidiuretic substance)
SIAD patients are subclinically volume-expanded, not truly euvolemic, due to 'AVP escape' mechanisms
Serum uric acid is often low (<4 mg/dL) in SIAD, reflecting suppressed proximal tubular transport
items.reference: HYPONATREMIA: "Four distinct patterns of AVP secretion have been recognized in patients with SIAD, independent for the most part of the underlying cause. Unregulated, erratic AVP secretion is seen in about a third of patients, with no obvious correlation between serum osmolality and circulating AVP levels. Other patients fail to suppress AVP secretion at lower serum osmolalities, with a normal response curve to hyperosmolar conditions; others have a \"reset osmostat,\" with a lower threshold osmolality and a left-shifted osmotic response curve. Finally, the fourth subset of patients have essentially no detectable circulating AVP, suggesting either a gain in function in renal water reabsorption or a circulating antidiuretic substance that is distinct from AVP."

items.title: Why low dietary solute intake alone can cause hyponatremia (beer potomania)
items.content:
Hyponatremia from low solute intake — classically 'beer potomania' in patients whose primary nutrient is beer (very low in protein and salt, ~1-2 mM Na+) — occurs even without AVP excess.
The fundamental defect is inadequate dietary solute intake, which limits the amount of urine solute available to 'carry' excreted water. Because the kidney cannot excrete free water without accompanying solute, even modest polydipsia overwhelms the reduced solute-driven water excretion and produces hyponatremia.
Patients typically present with a very low urine osmolality (<100-200 mOsm/kg) and urine Na+ <10-20 mM, distinguishing this from AVP-driven SIAD. The same picture can occur in nonalcoholic patients on extreme low-protein/vegetarian diets.
This is clinically important because resumption of a normal diet and/or saline hydration corrects the urinary solute deficit, often leading to overly rapid correction of plasma Na+ — these patients are at high risk for osmotic demyelination.
keyPoints: Beer potomania: very low protein/salt intake limits urinary solute excretion, restricting free-water excretion despite normal AVP suppressibility
Typical findings: urine osmolality <100-200 mOsm/kg, urine Na+ <10-20 mM
Can also occur with extreme low-solute (e.g., vegetarian) diets in nonalcoholic patients
Resumption of a normal diet/saline corrects rapidly — high risk of overcorrection and osmotic demyelination
items.reference: HYPONATREMIA: "Patients with hyponatremia due to low solute intake typically present with a very low urine osmolality (<100–200 mOsm/kg) with an urine Na⁺ concentration that is <10–20 mM. The fundamental abnormality is the inadequate dietary intake of solutes; the reduced urinary solute excretion limits water excretion such that hyponatremia ensues after relatively modest polydipsia."

items.title: How osmotic demyelination syndrome develops when chronic hyponatremia is corrected too quickly
items.content:
In chronic hyponatremia, brain cells have adapted by extruding organic osmolytes (creatine, betaine, glutamate, myoinositol, taurine) to reduce intracellular osmolality and limit cerebral edema.
When plasma Na+ is corrected too fast — by more than 8-10 mM in the first 24 hours or more than 18 mM in the first 48 hours — the brain cannot reaccumulate these osmolytes quickly enough. The resulting hypertonic stress on astrocytes triggers protein ubiquitination, endoplasmic reticulum stress (unfolded protein response), and apoptotic/autophagic cell death, plus disruption of the blood-brain barrier that allows immune mediator entry.
The pons is especially vulnerable (central pontine myelinolysis), producing a delayed presentation — often 1 or more days after overcorrection — with paraparesis/quadriparesis, dysphagia, dysarthria, diplopia, a 'locked-in syndrome,' or loss of consciousness. Extrapontine lesions (cerebellum, lateral geniculate body, thalamus, putamen, cortex) can cause ataxia, mutism, parkinsonism, dystonia, or catatonia.
Risk factors beyond rapid correction include alcoholism, malnutrition, hypokalemia, and liver transplantation — even appropriately slow correction can cause ODS in these patients.
keyPoints: Chronic hyponatremia: brain cells extrude organic osmolytes (creatine, betaine, glutamate, myoinositol, taurine) to reduce cerebral edema
Overcorrection (>8-10 mM/24h or >18 mM/48h) outpaces osmolyte reaccumulation, causing hypertonic astrocyte injury and ODS
Central pontine myelinolysis presents with delayed para/quadriparesis, dysarthria, diplopia, or a 'locked-in syndrome'
Risk factors: alcoholism, malnutrition, hypokalemia, liver transplantation — ODS can occur even with appropriately slow correction in these patients
items.reference: HYPONATREMIA: "Overly rapid correction of hyponatremia (>8–10 mM in 24 h or 18 mM in 48 h) causes hypertonic stress in astrocytes within brain regions prone to ODS, leading to generalized protein ubiquitination and endoplasmic reticulum stress due to activation of the unfolded protein response; this is accompanied by apoptotic and autophagic cell death... clinically, patients with central pontine myelinolysis can present 1 or more days after overcorrection of hyponatremia with para-paresis or quadriparesis, dysphagia, dysarthria, diplopia, a \"locked-in syndrome,\" and/or loss of consciousness."

items.title: Why acute and chronic hyponatremia call for different correction-rate targets
items.content:
The therapy of hyponatremia is guided by symptom severity, duration, and the predictability of the response to treatment.
Patients with acute hyponatremia (present <48 h) can develop severe symptoms — headache, nausea, vomiting, seizures, obtundation, or herniation — and tolerate, and indeed need, a more rapid initial correction to relieve cerebral edema.
Patients with chronic hyponatremia (>48 h) are less likely to have severe symptoms but are at risk for ODS if corrected too fast — the safe target is less than 6-8 mM in the first 24 hours and less than 6 mM in each subsequent 24-hour period, with even lower targets in patients at particular ODS risk (alcoholics, hypokalemic patients).
Because the response to hypertonic saline, isotonic saline, or AVP antagonists can be highly unpredictable, plasma Na+ must be monitored frequently (every 2-4 h during active correction) so therapy can be adjusted in real time.
keyPoints: Acute hyponatremia (<48h) can present with severe neurologic symptoms and tolerates more rapid initial correction
Chronic hyponatremia (>48h) correction should be <6-8 mM in the first 24h and <6 mM per subsequent 24h to avoid ODS
Lower correction targets apply to alcoholics, malnourished, or hypokalemic patients (higher ODS risk)
Frequent plasma Na+ monitoring (q2-4h during active therapy) is essential because the response is unpredictable
items.reference: TREATMENT Hyponatremia: "patients with chronic hyponatremia are at risk for ODS if plasma Na⁺ concentration is corrected by >8–10 mM within the first 24 h and/or by >18 mM within the first 48 h... The rate of correction should be comparatively slow in chronic hyponatremia (<6–8 mM in the first 24 h and <6 mM each subsequent 24 h) so as to avoid ODS; lower target rates are appropriate in patients at particular risk for ODS, such as alcoholics or hypokalemic patients."

items.title: How acute symptomatic hyponatremia is treated with a hypertonic saline bolus
items.content:
Acute symptomatic hyponatremia is a medical emergency, and treatment centers on hypertonic 3% saline (513 mM Na+) to rapidly raise plasma Na+ by a modest 1-2 mM/h, up to a total increase of 4-6 mM — usually enough to relieve severe acute symptoms.
A 100-mL bolus of hypertonic saline is more effective than a slow infusion, producing faster improvement in both serum sodium and mental status.
For ongoing infusions, the rate can be estimated from a calculated Na+ deficit (0.6 x body weight x [target Na+ - starting Na+]), but because the actual response can change rapidly with the underlying physiology, plasma Na+ must be checked every 2-4 hours during treatment and the infusion adjusted accordingly.
Supplemental oxygen/ventilatory support is also important, since acute hyponatremia can cause noncardiogenic pulmonary edema or hypercapneic respiratory failure; IV loop diuretics help both the pulmonary edema and free-water excretion. AVP antagonists (vaptans) have no approved role in acute hyponatremia.
keyPoints: 3% hypertonic saline raises plasma Na+ by 1-2 mM/h to a total of 4-6 mM to relieve acute severe symptoms
A 100-mL bolus is more effective than a slow infusion for acute symptomatic hyponatremia
Plasma Na+ must be checked every 2-4 h during treatment given the unpredictable response
Vaptans have no approved role in acute hyponatremia; loop diuretics help concurrent pulmonary edema
items.reference: TREATMENT Hyponatremia: "Treatment of acute symptomatic hyponatremia should include hypertonic 3% saline (513 mM) to acutely increase plasma Na⁺ concentration by 1–2 mM/h to a total of 4–6 mM; this modest increase is typically sufficient to alleviate severe acute symptoms, after which corrective guidelines for chronic hyponatremia are appropriate (see below). A bolus of 100 mL of hypertonic saline is more effective than an infusion, rapidly improving both serum sodium and mental status... AVP antagonists do not have an approved role in the management of acute hyponatremia."

items.title: Why the work-up of euvolemic hyponatremia must exclude hypothyroidism and secondary adrenal insufficiency before labeling it SIAD
items.content:
Although SIAD is the most common cause of euvolemic hyponatremia, two endocrine causes must be actively excluded because they are treatable with hormone replacement rather than fluid restriction or AVP antagonism.
Moderate-to-severe hypothyroidism can cause euvolemic hyponatremia that corrects once a euthyroid state is restored.
Secondary adrenal insufficiency from pituitary disease causes euvolemic (not hypovolemic) hyponatremia, because it is the glucocorticoid deficiency — not aldosterone deficiency — that predominates; glucocorticoids normally exert negative feedback on AVP release, so hydrocortisone replacement can rapidly normalize the AVP response and correct the hyponatremia. This contrasts with primary adrenal insufficiency, where aldosterone deficiency produces hypovolemic hyponatremia.
A cosyntropin stimulation test is required to assess for primary adrenal insufficiency in this work-up.
keyPoints: Hypothyroidism can cause euvolemic hyponatremia that resolves with restoration of euthyroidism
Secondary adrenal insufficiency (pituitary disease) causes euvolemic hyponatremia via glucocorticoid deficiency's loss of negative feedback on AVP
Primary adrenal insufficiency instead causes hypovolemic hyponatremia via aldosterone deficiency
A cosyntropin stimulation test is needed to assess for primary adrenal insufficiency
items.reference: HYPONATREMIA: "Euvolemic hyponatremia can occur in moderate to severe hypothyroidism, with correction after achieving a euthyroid state. Severe hyponatremia can also be a consequence of secondary adrenal insufficiency due to pituitary disease; whereas the deficit in circulating aldosterone in primary adrenal insufficiency causes hypovolemic hyponatremia, the predominant glucocorticoid deficiency in secondary adrenal failure is associated with euvolemic hyponatremia. Glucocorticoids exert a negative feedback on AVP release by the posterior pituitary such that hydrocortisone replacement in these patients can rapidly normalize the AVP response to osmolality, reducing circulating AVP."

items.title: Hypernatremia reflects a combined water and electrolyte deficit with water loss exceeding sodium loss
items.content:
Hypernatremia is defined as a plasma Na+ concentration >145 mM. It is considerably less common than hyponatremia but carries a much higher mortality — as high as 40-60% — largely reflecting the severity of underlying illness in affected patients (often elderly, hospitalized, or critically ill).
The core mechanism is usually a combined water and electrolyte deficit in which water losses exceed Na+ losses, rather than pure Na+ gain. This can arise from reduced water intake (impaired thirst or access to water), increased water loss (renal — DI/osmotic diuresis, or extrarenal — GI, skin, respiratory), or rarely from a Na+ load that outpaces water intake.
keyPoints: Hypernatremia = plasma Na+ >145 mM, with mortality rates as high as 40-60%
Usually reflects a combined water and electrolyte deficit, with H2O loss exceeding Na+ loss
Causes include impaired thirst/access to water, renal water loss (DI, osmotic diuresis), and extrarenal water loss
items.reference: HYPERNATREMIA: "Etiology Hypernatremia is defined as an increase in the plasma Na⁺ concentration to >145 mM. Considerably less common than hyponatremia, hypernatremia is nonetheless associated with mortality rates of as high as 40–60%... Hypernatremia is usually the result of a combined water and electrolyte deficit, with losses of H₂O in excess of Na⁺."

items.title: Why elderly patients and those with hypothalamic osmoreceptor defects are at highest risk for hypernatremia
items.content:
The most common predisposing factor for hypernatremia is simply reduced thirst and/or reduced access to water — a major reason elderly, hospitalized, or cognitively impaired patients are at highest risk.
A rarer but important cause is a central defect in hypothalamic osmoreceptor function, producing a combination of decreased thirst (adipsia) and reduced AVP secretion — 'adipsic DI.' Recognized causes include primary or metastatic hypothalamic tumors, occlusion or ligation of the anterior communicating artery (e.g., after aneurysm repair), trauma, hydrocephalus, and infiltrative/inflammatory disease such as sarcoidosis.
Because both the thirst drive and the AVP response are blunted, these patients do not mount the usual protective response to a rising plasma osmolality and can develop severe, recurrent hypernatremia.
keyPoints: Reduced thirst and/or reduced access to fluids is the most common risk factor for hypernatremia, especially in the elderly
Adipsic DI = combined defect in thirst and AVP secretion from hypothalamic osmoreceptor injury
Causes of adipsic DI: hypothalamic tumors, anterior communicating artery occlusion/ligation, trauma, hydrocephalus, sarcoidosis
items.reference: HYPERNATREMIA: "Elderly individuals with reduced thirst and/or diminished access to fluids are at the highest risk of developing hypernatremia. Patients with hypernatremia may rarely have a central defect in hypothalamic osmoreceptor function, with a mixture of both decreased thirst and reduced AVP secretion. Causes of this adipic DI include primary or metastatic tumor, occlusion or ligation of the anterior communicating artery, trauma, hydrocephalus, and inflammation such as sarcoidosis."

items.title: How AVP/copeptin levels and the DDAVP response distinguish central from nephrogenic DI
items.content:
When a hypernatremic patient has an inappropriately dilute urine, the key diagnostic question is whether AVP secretion or AVP action is the problem.
In nephrogenic DI (NDI), the kidney cannot respond to AVP, so circulating AVP and copeptin levels are appropriately high (the body is trying to compensate) — but the urine osmolality fails to rise meaningfully after DDAVP, increasing by less than 50% or less than 150 mOsm/kg from baseline.
In central DI, the defect is in AVP secretion itself, so baseline AVP/copeptin are low — but administering DDAVP (which bypasses the secretory defect) produces a robust increase in urine osmolality, confirming the renal response machinery is intact.
This AVP/copeptin level plus DDAVP-response pairing is the central diagnostic test that separates the two major categories of DI and directs therapy (desmopressin for central DI vs. addressing the renal cause for NDI).
keyPoints: NDI: high baseline AVP/copeptin, but urine osmolality rises <50% (or <150 mOsm/kg) after DDAVP
Central DI: low baseline AVP/copeptin, with a robust urine-concentrating response to DDAVP
This pairing of AVP/copeptin levels with the DDAVP response is the key test distinguishing central from nephrogenic DI
items.reference: HYPERNATREMIA: "Hypernatremic patients with NDI will have high serum levels of AVP and copeptin. Their low urine osmolality will also fail to respond to DDAVP, increasing by <50% or <150 mOsm/kg from baseline; patients with central DI will respond to DDAVP, with a reduced circulating AVP and copeptin."

items.title: Why lithium causes nephrogenic DI through ENaC entry, and how amiloride helps — but only up to a point
items.content:
Lithium is one of the most common causes of acquired NDI. It acts through multiple mechanisms, including direct inhibition of renal glycogen synthase kinase-3 (GSK3), which impairs AQP2-mediated water reabsorption in collecting-duct principal cells.
Critically, lithium must enter principal cells through the amiloride-sensitive epithelial Na+ channel (ENaC) to exert this effect. Because amiloride blocks ENaC, co-administering amiloride with lithium can reduce lithium entry into principal cells and mitigate lithium-associated NDI — a clinically useful combination for patients who require ongoing lithium therapy.
However, prolonged lithium use also causes chronic tubulointerstitial scarring and chronic kidney disease. Once this structural damage has occurred, NDI can persist long after lithium is stopped, and the therapeutic benefit of amiloride is correspondingly reduced — underscoring the importance of monitoring renal function and urine-concentrating ability during long-term lithium therapy.
keyPoints: Lithium causes NDI partly via inhibition of renal GSK3, impairing AQP2-mediated water reabsorption
Lithium must enter principal cells through ENaC — amiloride (an ENaC blocker) can reduce lithium entry and mitigate NDI
Chronic lithium use causes tubulointerstitial scarring/CKD, after which NDI can persist post-lithium with reduced amiloride benefit
items.reference: HYPERNATREMIA: "Lithium causes NDI by multiple mechanisms, including direct inhibition of renal glycogen synthase kinase-3 (GSK3)... The entry of lithium through the amiloride-sensitive Na⁺ channel ENaC (Fig. 56-4) is required for the effect of the drug on principal cells, such that combined therapy within lithium and amiloride can mitigate lithium-associated NDI. However, lithium causes chronic tubulointerstitial scarring and chronic kidney disease after prolonged therapy, such that patients may have a persistent NDI long after stopping the drug, with a reduced therapeutic benefit from amiloride."

items.title: How the free-water deficit is calculated and corrected over 48-72 hours
items.content:
Correcting hypernatremia requires replacing the free-water deficit gradually, since overly rapid correction risks cerebral edema (the inverse problem to ODS in hyponatremia).
The standard approach: (1) estimate total-body water (TBW) as 50% of body weight in women and 60% in men; (2) calculate the free-water deficit as [(serum Na+ - 140)/140] x TBW; (3) administer this deficit over 48-72 hours, limiting the decrease in plasma Na+ to no more than 10 mM per 24 hours.
This formula gives the volume of pure water needed to bring plasma Na+ from its current value down to 140 mM; the actual infusate (D5W, hypotonic saline, etc.) and rate are then chosen so that the effective free-water delivery matches this calculated deficit over the targeted time frame, with frequent reassessment of plasma Na+.
keyPoints: TBW estimate: 50% of body weight in women, 60% in men
Free-water deficit = [(serum Na+ - 140)/140] x TBW
Replace the deficit over 48-72 h, limiting the fall in plasma Na+ to <10 mM per 24 h
items.reference: TABLE 56-3 Management of Hypernatremia: "1. Estimate total-body water (TBW): 50% of body weight in women and 60% in men 2. Calculate free-water deficit: [Na⁺ - 140]/140 × TBW 3. Administer deficit over 48–72 h, without decrease in plasma Na⁺ concentration by >10 mM/24 h"

items.title: Why gestational DI develops late in pregnancy and why DDAVP (not vasopressin) is the treatment
items.content:
Gestational DI is a rare cause of polyuria and hypernatremia specific to late-term pregnancy. The placenta produces a protease with 'vasopressinase' activity that degrades circulating AVP, lowering effective AVP levels and producing a DI-like picture.
Because the problem is accelerated degradation of native AVP rather than a structural defect in AVP secretion or renal response, the treatment is DDAVP (desmopressin) — a synthetic AVP analogue that is resistant to the placental vasopressinase enzyme and therefore remains effective despite the high enzyme activity.
The condition typically resolves after delivery, when placental vasopressinase activity disappears.
keyPoints: Gestational DI results from a placental 'vasopressinase' protease that degrades circulating AVP late in pregnancy
DDAVP is effective because it resists degradation by the vasopressinase enzyme, unlike native AVP
The condition is specific to late-term pregnancy and resolves after delivery
items.reference: HYPERNATREMIA: "gestational DI is a rare complication of late-term pregnancy wherein increased activity of a circulating placental protease with \"vasopressinase\" activity leads to reduced circulating AVP and polyuria, often accompanied by hypernatremia. DDAVP is an effective therapy for this syndrome, given its resistance to the vasopressinase enzyme."

items.title: Hypokalemia is common, carries a tenfold mortality increase, and arises from redistribution or true potassium loss
items.content:
Hypokalemia, defined as plasma K+ <3.5 mM, occurs in up to 20% of hospitalized patients and is associated with a roughly tenfold increase in in-hospital mortality — driven mainly by adverse effects on cardiac rhythm and blood pressure, as well as broader cardiovascular morbidity.
Mechanistically, hypokalemia arises from one of two broad processes: redistribution of K+ from the ECF into cells (without a true total-body deficit), or true loss of K+ from the body via renal or nonrenal (e.g., GI) routes.
Distinguishing redistribution from true loss is the first branch point in the diagnostic approach, since redistributive causes (e.g., insulin, beta-agonists, alkalosis, periodic paralysis) resolve once the underlying trigger is addressed, whereas true-loss causes require identifying and treating the source of K+ loss plus replacement of the deficit.
keyPoints: Hypokalemia = plasma K+ <3.5 mM, present in up to 20% of hospitalized patients
Associated with a ~10-fold increase in in-hospital mortality via cardiac rhythm/BP effects
Two broad mechanisms: redistribution of K+ into cells, or true renal/nonrenal K+ loss
items.reference: HYPOKALEMIA: "Hypokalemia, defined as a plasma K⁺ concentration of <3.5 mM, occurs in up to 20% of hospitalized patients. Hypokalemia is associated with a tenfold increase in in-hospital mortality, due to adverse effects on cardiac rhythm, blood pressure, and cardiovascular morbidity. Mechanistically, hypokalemia can be caused by redistribution of K⁺ between tissues and the ECF or by renal and nonrenal loss of K⁺."

items.title: Why thyrotoxic periodic paralysis causes profound nocturnal hypokalemia and responds to propranolol
items.content:
Thyrotoxic periodic paralysis (TPP) is a redistributive cause of severe hypokalemia that occurs disproportionately in patients of Asian or Latin American descent with hyperthyroidism.
Patients present with weakness of the extremities and limb girdles, with paralytic episodes occurring most frequently between 1 and 6 a.m. — often precipitated by a high-carbohydrate meal or strenuous exercise the prior evening, both of which trigger insulin release.
The hypokalemia results from both direct and indirect activation of the Na+/K+-ATPase, driving K+ into cells. Because catecholamines (via beta-adrenergic stimulation) contribute to this activation, high-dose propranolol (3 mg/kg) rapidly reverses the hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia, and paralysis — making it both a diagnostic and therapeutic maneuver that avoids the rebound hyperkalemia risk of aggressive K+ replacement.
keyPoints: TPP occurs more often in patients of Asian or Latin American origin with hyperthyroidism
Paralytic episodes cluster between 1 and 6 a.m., often after a high-carbohydrate meal or exercise
High-dose propranolol (3 mg/kg) rapidly reverses the hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia, and paralysis of TPP
items.reference: HYPOKALEMIA: "TPP develops more frequently in patients of Asian or Latin American origin... Patients with TPP typically present with weakness of the extremities and limb girdles, with paralytic episodes that occur most frequently between 1 and 6 a.m... The hypokalemia in TPP is also attributed to both direct and indirect activation of the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase... high-dose propranolol (3 mg/kg) rapidly reverses the associated hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia, and paralysis."

items.title: How magnesium deficiency causes treatment-refractory hypokalemia
items.content:
Magnesium deficiency is an important and often-overlooked cause of hypokalemia that is resistant to potassium replacement alone.
Mechanistically, magnesium depletion inhibits muscle Na+/K+-ATPase activity, reducing K+ influx into cells and causing a secondary kaliuresis (renal K+ wasting). Separately, magnesium depletion causes exaggerated K+ secretion by the distal nephron, compounding the renal loss.
As a result, hypomagnesemic patients are clinically refractory to K+ replacement until magnesium is also repleted — any clinician managing persistent hypokalemia despite adequate K+ supplementation should check (and correct) serum magnesium.
keyPoints: Magnesium depletion inhibits muscle Na+/K+-ATPase and causes a secondary kaliuresis
Magnesium depletion also directly increases distal nephron K+ secretion
Hypomagnesemic patients are refractory to K+ replacement until Mg2+ is also corrected
items.reference: HYPOKALEMIA: "Magnesium depletion has inhibitory effects on muscle Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase activity, reducing influx into muscle cells and causing a secondary kaliuresis. In addition, magnesium depletion causes exaggerated K⁺ secretion by the distal nephron... In consequence, hypomagnesemic patients are clinically refractory to K⁺ replacement in the absence of Mg²⁺ repletion."

items.title: Why urine chloride helps distinguish vomiting/bulimia, diuretic abuse, and Gitelman syndrome as causes of hypokalemic alkalosis
items.content:
The three most common causes of chronic hypokalemic alkalosis are surreptitious (self-induced) vomiting, diuretic abuse, and Gitelman syndrome (GS) — and urinary electrolyte patterns help separate them when the history is unreliable.
In bulimia with surreptitious vomiting, the urine Cl- is typically very low (<10 mmol/L), because volume depletion from vomiting drives avid renal Cl- reabsorption — distinguishing it from the other two causes, where renal Cl- handling is intrinsically impaired.
In Gitelman syndrome, urinary Na+, K+, and Cl- are persistently and consistently elevated, reflecting the fixed defect in the thiazide-sensitive NaCl cotransporter. In diuretic abuse, urinary electrolytes are also elevated but tend to be less markedly so and show greater variability from sample to sample, reflecting intermittent diuretic use rather than a fixed transport defect.
keyPoints: Surreptitious vomiting/bulimia: urine Cl- <10 mmol/L (volume depletion drives Cl- retention)
Gitelman syndrome: urine Na+, K+, Cl- persistently and consistently elevated (fixed NCC defect)
Diuretic abuse: urine electrolytes elevated but less markedly and more variable (intermittent use)
items.reference: HYPOKALEMIA: "The most common causes of chronic hypokalemic alkalosis are surreptitious vomiting, diuretic abuse, and GS; these can be distinguished by the pattern of urinary electrolytes. Hypokalemic patients with vomiting due to bulimia will thus typically have a urinary Cl⁻<10 mmol/L; urine Na⁺, K⁺, and Cl⁻ are persistently elevated in GS... but less elevated in diuretic abuse and with greater variability."

items.title: How the magnitude of the total-body potassium deficit is estimated and why replacement must be gradual
items.content:
When hypokalemia reflects a true total-body K+ deficit (not redistribution), the size of the deficit correlates roughly with the serum K+ value: serum K+ falls by about 0.27 mM for every 100 mmol reduction in total-body K+ stores, so a loss of 400-800 mmol of total-body K+ lowers serum K+ by roughly 2.0 mM.
This relationship lets clinicians estimate (very roughly) how much K+ replacement is needed to correct a given deficit. However, because intracellular K+ stores re-equilibrate slowly, this deficit must be replaced gradually over 24-48 hours, with frequent monitoring of plasma K+ — replacing it too quickly risks transient overrepletion and iatrogenic hyperkalemia before the administered K+ has fully redistributed into cells.
keyPoints: Serum K+ falls ~0.27 mM per 100 mmol reduction in total-body K+ stores (true deficit, not redistribution)
A 400-800 mmol total-body K+ deficit lowers serum K+ by roughly 2.0 mM
Replacement must occur gradually over 24-48 h with frequent monitoring to avoid transient overcorrection/hyperkalemia
items.reference: TREATMENT Hypokalemia: "In the absence of abnormal K⁺ redistribution, the total deficit correlates with serum K⁺, such that serum K⁺ drops by ~0.27 mM for every 100-mmol reduction in total-body stores; loss of 400–800 mmol of total-body K⁺ results in a reduction in serum K⁺ by ~2.0 mM. Notably, given the delay in redistributing potassium into intracellular compartments, this deficit must be replaced gradually over 24–48 h, with frequent monitoring of plasma K⁺ concentration to avoid transient overrepletion and transient hyperkalemia."

items.title: Hyperkalemia affects up to 10% of hospitalized patients and most often reflects reduced renal potassium excretion
items.content:
Hyperkalemia is defined as a plasma K+ level >5.5 mM, occurring in up to 10% of hospitalized patients; severe hyperkalemia (>6.0 mM) occurs in about 1% of patients and carries a significantly increased mortality risk.
Although acute redistribution (K+ shifting out of cells) and reduced cellular K+ uptake can cause hyperkalemia, the most frequent underlying cause is a decrease in renal K+ excretion — whether from reduced GFR, reduced distal Na+ delivery, hypoaldosteronism, or drugs that impair tubular K+ secretion.
This predominance of a renal-excretion defect is why the diagnostic approach to hyperkalemia focuses heavily on renal function, the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis, and medications.
keyPoints: Hyperkalemia = plasma K+ >5.5 mM (up to 10% of hospitalized patients); severe hyperkalemia >6.0 mM occurs in ~1% with increased mortality
Redistribution and reduced cellular uptake can cause acute hyperkalemia
Reduced renal K+ excretion is the most frequent underlying cause overall
items.reference: HYPERKALEMIA: "Hyperkalemia is defined as a plasma potassium level of 5.5 mM, occurring in up to 10% of hospitalized patients; severe hyperkalemia (>6.0 mM) occurs in ~1%, with a significantly increased risk of mortality. Although redistribution and reduced tissue uptake can acutely cause hyperkalemia, a decrease in renal K⁺ excretion is the most frequent underlying cause."

items.title: Why drugs that block the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis are predictable, common causes of hyperkalemia
items.content:
Medication-associated hyperkalemia is extremely common in clinical practice, and most culprit drugs act by inhibiting some component of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) — the body's principal mechanism for promoting renal K+ excretion via aldosterone-driven distal tubular K+ secretion.
ACE inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs), direct renin inhibitors, and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (MRAs, e.g., spironolactone, eplerenone) all reduce aldosterone activity (or its effects) and are predictable, common causes of hyperkalemia — particularly when two or more of these agents are prescribed together, since their hyperkalemic effects compound.
This predictability means combination RAAS-blocking regimens (e.g., an ACE inhibitor plus an MRA) require closer monitoring of serum K+, especially in patients with reduced GFR.
keyPoints: Most hyperkalemia-causing drugs inhibit some part of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis
ACE inhibitors, ARBs, renin inhibitors, and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists are predictable, common causes
Combining RAAS-blocking drugs compounds the hyperkalemia risk and requires closer K+ monitoring
items.reference: HYPERKALEMIA: "Most medications associated with hyperkalemia cause inhibition of some component of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis. ACE inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, renin inhibitors, and MLRs are predictable and common causes of hyperkalemia, particularly when prescribed in combination."

items.title: How the transtubular potassium gradient (TTKG) is used to assess the renal response to hyperkalemia
items.content:
The transtubular K+ gradient (TTKG) is a calculation that estimates the K+ concentration gradient across the cortical collecting duct, using paired serum and urine osmolality and K+ values: TTKG = (urine K+ x serum osmolality) / (serum K+ x urine osmolality).
The TTKG provides a rough index of distal nephron K+ secretion relative to the prevailing serum K+. Based on historical data, expected values are <3 in the presence of hypokalemia (i.e., the kidney is appropriately retaining K+) and >7-8 in the presence of hyperkalemia (i.e., the kidney is appropriately excreting K+).
A TTKG that falls outside the expected range for the patient's serum K+ — e.g., a TTKG <7 in a hyperkalemic patient — suggests an impaired renal K+ excretory response (such as hypoaldosteronism or a tubular defect) rather than simply excess K+ intake or redistribution.
keyPoints: TTKG = (urine K+ x serum osmolality) / (serum K+ x urine osmolality)
Expected TTKG <3 with hypokalemia and >7-8 with hyperkalemia, reflecting appropriate renal K+ handling
A TTKG outside the expected range for the serum K+ suggests an impaired renal K+ excretory response
items.reference: HYPERKALEMIA: "Serum and urine osmolality are required for calculation of the transtubular K⁺ gradient (TTKG)... The expected values of the TTKG are largely based on historical data, and are <3 in the presence of hypokalemia and >7–8 in the presence of hyperkalemia."

items.title: Why intravenous calcium is given first in severe hyperkalemia, and how it works
items.content:
The treatment of severe hyperkalemia follows a staged approach: (1) stabilize the cardiac membrane, (2) shift K+ into cells, and (3) remove K+ from the body. Intravenous calcium is the first step because it acts within minutes to protect the heart from the membrane effects of hyperkalemia.
Mechanistically, hyperkalemia depolarizes the resting membrane potential toward the threshold potential, reducing the difference between them and impairing normal depolarization (a 'depolarization blockade'). Calcium raises the threshold potential itself — without changing the resting membrane potential — thereby restoring the normal gap between resting and threshold potentials and reversing the depolarization blockade, even though it does not lower serum K+.
The second stage (insulin with dextrose, plus nebulized albuterol) shifts K+ into cells to produce a more durable reduction in serum K+, while the third stage (potassium binders or dialysis) achieves definitive removal of excess K+ from the body.
keyPoints: Severe hyperkalemia treatment is staged: (1) cardiac membrane stabilization, (2) shift K+ intracellularly, (3) remove K+ from the body
IV calcium acts within minutes by raising the threshold potential, reversing the depolarization blockade — without lowering serum K+
Insulin/dextrose and albuterol (stage 2) shift K+ into cells; binders/dialysis (stage 3) remove K+ from the body
items.reference: TREATMENT Hyperkalemia: "Intravenous calcium serves to protect the heart... Calcium raises the action potential threshold and reduces excitability, without changing the resting membrane potential. By restoring the difference between resting and threshold potentials, calcium reverses the depolarization blockade due to hyperkalemia."

items.title: How newer potassium binders (patiromer, sodium zirconium cyclosilicate) differ from sodium polystyrene sulfonate
items.content:
For definitive removal of excess potassium, intestinal potassium binders trap dietary and secreted K+ in the gut lumen so it is excreted in stool rather than absorbed.
Two newer agents — patiromer and sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (SZC) — are now preferred over the older agent sodium polystyrene sulfonate (SPS) because they lack SPS's associated intestinal toxicity (including reports of intestinal necrosis, particularly when given with sorbitol).
Patiromer is a nonabsorbed polymer given as a powder for suspension that binds K+ in exchange for releasing Ca2+ in the gut. SZC is an inorganic, nonabsorbable crystalline compound that binds K+ and NH4+ in exchange for Na+ and H+ in the intestine. Both are used primarily for chronic/outpatient management of hyperkalemia (e.g., to allow continuation of RAAS-blocking drugs) rather than for acute, emergent lowering of serum K+.
keyPoints: Patiromer and sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (SZC) are preferred over SPS due to lower intestinal toxicity risk
Patiromer: nonabsorbed polymer that exchanges K+ for Ca2+ in the gut
SZC: nonabsorbable crystalline compound exchanging K+/NH4+ for Na+/H+ in the gut
Both are primarily for chronic hyperkalemia management, not acute emergent lowering of serum K+
items.reference: TREATMENT Hyperkalemia: "Two other intestinal potassium binders are available for the management of hyperkalemia. These agents lack the intestinal toxicity of SPS and are preferred over SPS for the management of hyperkalemia. Patiromer is a nonabsorbed polymer provided as a powder for suspension, which binds K⁺ in exchange for Ca²⁺... Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate is an inorganic, nonabsorbable crystalline compound that exchanges both Na⁺ and H⁺ ions in exchange for K⁺ and NH₄⁺ in the intestine."

items.question: A 28-year-old woman with bulimia presents with weakness and dizziness. Labs show hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, and a urine sodium of 35 mEq/L. Which urinary measurement would best confirm that she is volume-depleted?
items.explanation: Bicarbonaturia from the metabolic alkalosis obligates urinary Na+ excretion even when the patient is hypovolemic, so urine Na+ can be misleadingly elevated. Urine Cl- <25 mM remains a reliable indicator of volume depletion in this setting.
items.reference: HYPOVOLEMIA: "the urine Na⁺ concentration is typically <20 mM in nonrenal causes of hypovolemia, with a urine osmolality of >450 mOsm/kg... patients with hypovolemia and a hypochloremic alkalosis due to vomiting, diarrhea, or diuretics will typically have a urine Na⁺ concentration >20 mM and urine pH of >7.0, due to the increase in filtered HCO₃⁻; the urine Cl⁻ concentration in this setting is a more accurate indicator of volume status, with a level <25 mM suggestive of hypovolemia."

items.question: A patient in hemorrhagic shock has a measured plasma osmolality of 278 mOsm/kg (below the typical ~285 mOsm/kg threshold) but markedly elevated serum AVP. Which mechanism best explains this finding?
options: Hypovolemia lowers the osmotic threshold for AVP release and steepens the slope of the AVP-osmolality response curve
Hypervolemia raises the osmotic threshold for AVP release
AVP secretion is entirely independent of extracellular volume status
An osmolality below 285 mOsm/kg always suppresses AVP secretion regardless of volume
items.explanation: ECF volume strongly modulates AVP secretion: hypovolemia lowers the osmotic threshold and increases the slope of the AVP response, so AVP rises even at a lower-than-normal osmolality in order to defend circulating volume.
items.reference: COMPOSITION OF BODY FLUIDS: "AVP secretion is stimulated as systemic osmolality increases above a threshold level of ~285 mOsm/kg, above which there is a linear relationship between osmolality and circulating AVP... ECF volume strongly modulates the relationship between circulating osmolality and AVP release, such that hypovolemia reduces the osmotic threshold and increases the slope of the response curve to osmolality; hypervolemia has an opposite effect, increasing the osmotic threshold and reducing the slope of the response curve."

items.question: A patient with central diabetes insipidus is given desmopressin (DDAVP). By what mechanism does the urine become concentrated within hours of treatment?
options: Increased glomerular filtration rate concentrates the filtrate
AVP-V2-receptor signaling triggers PKA-dependent insertion of aquaporin-2 water channels into the collecting-duct apical membrane
Decreased renal blood flow passively concentrates the urine
DDAVP directly stimulates the Na+/K+-ATPase in the loop of Henle
items.explanation: AVP (or DDAVP) binding to V2 receptors activates PKA-dependent phosphorylation and apical insertion of AQP2 in collecting-duct principal cells, allowing water to move down the medullary osmotic gradient and concentrating the urine.
items.reference: SODIUM AND WATER: "AVP-induced, PKA-dependent phosphorylation of the aquaporin-2 water channel in principal cells stimulates the insertion of active water channels into the lumen of the CD, resulting in transepithelial water absorption down the medullary osmotic gradient... Under \"antidiuretic\" conditions, with increased circulating AVP, the kidney reabsorbs water filtered by the glomerulus, equilibrating the osmolality across the CD epithelium to excrete a hypertonic, \"concentrated\" urine (osmolarity of up to 1200 mOsm/kg)."

items.question: A patient with poorly controlled diabetes presents with polyuria, dry mucous membranes, and a plasma glucose of 650 mg/dL. Which mechanism best explains the resulting volume depletion?
options: Osmotic diuresis from a high filtered glucose load impairing tubular Na+-Cl- and water reabsorption
Third-spacing of fluid into the peritoneal cavity
Increased AVP-mediated water reabsorption in the collecting duct
items.explanation: A high filtered load of glucose (or other endogenous solutes such as urea) impairs tubular Na+-Cl- and water reabsorption, producing an osmotic diuresis — a classic renal cause of hypovolemia.
items.reference: HYPOVOLEMIA: "RENAL CAUSES Excessive urinary Na⁺-Cl⁻ and water loss is a feature of several conditions. A high filtered load of endogenous solutes, such as glucose and urea, can impair tubular reabsorption of Na⁺-Cl⁻ and water, leading to an osmotic diuresis."

items.question: A normonatremic patient presents in hypovolemic shock after several days of vomiting and poor oral intake. Which intravenous fluid is the most appropriate initial resuscitation choice?
items.explanation: Isotonic 0.9% saline (154 mM Na+) is the most appropriate resuscitation fluid for normonatremic or hyponatremic patients with severe hypovolemia.
items.reference: TREATMENT Hypovolemia: "Isotonic, \"normal\" saline (0.9% NaCl, 154 mM Na⁺) is the most appropriate resuscitation fluid for normonatremic or hyponatremic patients with severe hypovolemia; colloid solutions such as intravenous albumin are not demonstrably superior for this purpose."

items.question: A hospitalized patient is incidentally found to have a plasma sodium of 128 mM. What is the most important next step in the diagnostic approach?
options: Immediately start hypertonic saline regardless of symptoms
Assess extracellular fluid volume status to classify the hyponatremia as hypovolemic, euvolemic, or hypervolemic
Order an emergency head CT before any other evaluation
items.explanation: Hyponatremia is diagnostically subdivided by volume status into hypovolemic, euvolemic, and hypervolemic categories, and this classification is the foundation for identifying the cause and choosing initial therapy.
items.reference: HYPONATREMIA: "Hyponatremia, which is defined as a plasma Na⁺ concentration <135 mM, is a very common disorder, occurring in up to 22% of hospitalized patients... Hyponatremia is thus subdivided diagnostically into three groups, depending on clinical history and volume status, i.e., \"hypovolemic,\" \"euvolemic,\" and \"hyper-volemic\" (Fig. 56-5)."

items.question: A patient with decompensated heart failure and significant peripheral edema has a plasma sodium of 129 mM despite a low effective circulating volume. What is the primary driver of this hyponatremia?
options: Nonosmotic AVP release driven by reduced effective arterial volume, leading to free-water retention
items.explanation: In hypervolemic states such as heart failure, reduced effective arterial volume ('arterial underfilling') triggers nonosmotic AVP release and renal water retention, producing hyponatremia despite total-body Na+ and water excess — the same AVP-driven mechanism that underlies most hyponatremia.
items.reference: HYPONATREMIA: "This disorder is almost always the result of an increase in circulating AVP and/or increased renal sensitivity to AVP, combined with an intake of free water; a notable exception is hyponatremia due to low solute intake (see below)."

items.question: A patient with small cell lung cancer has hyponatremia (Na 124 mM), a euvolemic exam, urine osmolality of 320 mOsm/kg, and urine Na+ of 45 mM. Thyroid and adrenal function are normal. Which pattern of AVP secretion is most consistent with SIAD in this setting?
options: Complete absence of detectable circulating AVP
AVP secretion that fails to suppress appropriately despite a low serum osmolality
AVP secretion strictly proportional to dietary solute intake
AVP secretion occurring only during episodes of hypoglycemia
items.explanation: One of the recognized patterns of SIAD is failure to suppress AVP secretion at low serum osmolalities (with an otherwise normal response curve to hyperosmolar stimuli), which — combined with ongoing free-water intake — produces euvolemic hyponatremia with an inappropriately concentrated urine.
items.reference: HYPONATREMIA: "Four distinct patterns of AVP secretion have been recognized in patients with SIAD, independent for the most part of the underlying cause. Unregulated, erratic AVP secretion is seen in about a third of patients, with no obvious correlation between serum osmolality and circulating AVP levels. Other patients fail to suppress AVP secretion at lower serum osmolalities, with a normal response curve to hyperosmolar conditions; others have a \"reset osmostat,\"... Finally, the fourth subset of patients have essentially no detectable circulating AVP, suggesting either a gain in function in renal water reabsorption or a circulating antidiuretic substance that is distinct from AVP."

items.question: A malnourished man whose diet consists mainly of beer presents with a plasma sodium of 118 mM, a urine osmolality of 90 mOsm/kg, and a urine sodium of 8 mM. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Hyponatremia due to low solute intake (beer potomania)
Primary polydipsia with a normal diet
items.explanation: A very low urine osmolality (<100-200 mOsm/kg) with a low urine Na+ (<10-20 mM) in the setting of a low-solute diet is characteristic of hyponatremia due to low solute intake (beer potomania) — here AVP is appropriately suppressible, but the reduced urinary solute limits free-water excretion.
items.reference: HYPONATREMIA: "Patients with hyponatremia due to low solute intake typically present with a very low urine osmolality (<100–200 mOsm/kg) with an urine Na⁺ concentration that is <10–20 mM. The fundamental abnormality is the inadequate dietary intake of solutes; the reduced urinary solute excretion limits water excretion such that hyponatremia ensues after relatively modest polydipsia."

items.question: A patient with chronic hyponatremia (Na 110 mM) has plasma sodium corrected to 122 mM over the first 24 hours. Two days later the patient develops dysarthria, quadriparesis, and a 'locked-in' appearance. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: New ischemic stroke unrelated to sodium correction
Osmotic demyelination syndrome from overly rapid correction of hyponatremia
Wernicke encephalopathy unrelated to sodium correction
items.explanation: Correction by 12 mM in 24 hours exceeds the safe limit (8-10 mM/24h) for chronic hyponatremia. The delayed presentation with quadriparesis, dysarthria, diplopia, and a locked-in syndrome is classic for central pontine myelinolysis (osmotic demyelination syndrome).
items.reference: HYPONATREMIA: "Overly rapid correction of hyponatremia (>8–10 mM in 24 h or 18 mM in 48 h) causes hypertonic stress in astrocytes within brain regions prone to ODS... clinically, patients with central pontine myelinolysis can present 1 or more days after overcorrection of hyponatremia with para-paresis or quadriparesis, dysphagia, dysarthria, diplopia, a \"locked-in syndrome,\" and/or loss of consciousness."

items.question: An asymptomatic patient with chronic hyponatremia (Na 116 mM) is started on isotonic saline. What is the maximum safe increase in plasma sodium over the first 24 hours to minimize the risk of osmotic demyelination?
options: There is no upper limit if the patient is asymptomatic
items.explanation: In chronic hyponatremia, correction should be limited to <6-8 mM in the first 24 hours and <6 mM in each subsequent 24-hour period to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome, with even more conservative targets in higher-risk patients (alcoholics, hypokalemic patients).
items.reference: TREATMENT Hyponatremia: "The rate of correction should be comparatively slow in chronic hyponatremia (<6–8 mM in the first 24 h and <6 mM each subsequent 24 h) so as to avoid ODS; lower target rates are appropriate in patients at particular risk for ODS, such as alcoholics or hypokalemic patients."

items.question: A patient develops acute hyponatremia (Na 118 mM) after a marathon, with new seizures and obtundation. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
items.explanation: Acute symptomatic hyponatremia is a medical emergency best treated with a bolus of hypertonic 3% saline, which is more effective than a slow infusion at rapidly improving both serum sodium and mental status.
items.reference: TREATMENT Hyponatremia: "Treatment of acute symptomatic hyponatremia should include hypertonic 3% saline (513 mM) to acutely increase plasma Na⁺ concentration by 1–2 mM/h to a total of 4–6 mM; this modest increase is typically sufficient to alleviate severe acute symptoms... A bolus of 100 mL of hypertonic saline is more effective than an infusion, rapidly improving both serum sodium and mental status."

items.question: A patient with a pituitary macroadenoma develops euvolemic hyponatremia. Morning cortisol is low with a low ACTH, consistent with secondary adrenal insufficiency. Which treatment is most likely to correct the hyponatremia?
items.explanation: Secondary adrenal insufficiency causes euvolemic hyponatremia through glucocorticoid deficiency, which removes a normal negative-feedback restraint on AVP release. Hydrocortisone replacement can rapidly normalize the AVP response to osmolality and correct the hyponatremia.
items.reference: HYPONATREMIA: "Severe hyponatremia can also be a consequence of secondary adrenal insufficiency due to pituitary disease; whereas the deficit in circulating aldosterone in primary adrenal insufficiency causes hypovolemic hyponatremia, the predominant glucocorticoid deficiency in secondary adrenal failure is associated with euvolemic hyponatremia. Glucocorticoids exert a negative feedback on AVP release by the posterior pituitary such that hydrocortisone replacement in these patients can rapidly normalize the AVP response to osmolality, reducing circulating AVP."

items.question: An elderly nursing home resident with a febrile illness and poor oral intake develops a plasma sodium of 156 mM. What is the most common underlying mechanism for this hypernatremia?
options: A combined water and electrolyte deficit with water loss exceeding sodium loss
items.explanation: Hypernatremia is usually the result of a combined water and electrolyte deficit in which losses of water exceed losses of sodium — commonly precipitated, as here, by reduced fluid intake plus insensible losses from fever.
items.reference: HYPERNATREMIA: "Etiology Hypernatremia is defined as an increase in the plasma Na⁺ concentration to >145 mM. Considerably less common than hyponatremia, hypernatremia is nonetheless associated with mortality rates of as high as 40–60%... Hypernatremia is usually the result of a combined water and electrolyte deficit, with losses of H₂O in excess of Na⁺."

items.question: A patient who underwent surgical repair of an anterior communicating artery aneurysm develops recurrent hypernatremia with an absent thirst response despite a markedly elevated plasma osmolality. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Adipsic DI from hypothalamic osmoreceptor injury
items.explanation: Injury near the anterior communicating artery is a recognized cause of adipsic DI, in which combined damage to hypothalamic osmoreceptors blunts both thirst and AVP secretion, so the patient fails to drink despite severe hypertonicity.
items.reference: HYPERNATREMIA: "Patients with hypernatremia may rarely have a central defect in hypothalamic osmoreceptor function, with a mixture of both decreased thirst and reduced AVP secretion. Causes of this adipic DI include primary or metastatic tumor, occlusion or ligation of the anterior communicating artery, trauma, hydrocephalus, and inflammation such as sarcoidosis."

items.question: A patient with polyuria and a plasma sodium of 152 mM has elevated serum AVP and copeptin levels. After administration of DDAVP, urine osmolality increases by only 20% from baseline. What is the most likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: High baseline AVP/copeptin with failure of urine osmolality to rise substantially (by <50% or <150 mOsm/kg) after DDAVP indicates that the kidney cannot respond to AVP — i.e., nephrogenic DI.
items.reference: HYPERNATREMIA: "Hypernatremic patients with NDI will have high serum levels of AVP and copeptin. Their low urine osmolality will also fail to respond to DDAVP, increasing by <50% or <150 mOsm/kg from baseline; patients with central DI will respond to DDAVP, with a reduced circulating AVP and copeptin."

items.question: A patient on long-term lithium therapy for bipolar disorder develops nephrogenic DI with polyuria. Which medication, by blocking the channel through which lithium enters collecting-duct principal cells, may help mitigate the NDI?
items.explanation: Lithium enters principal cells through the amiloride-sensitive ENaC channel. Co-administration of amiloride blocks this entry route and can mitigate lithium-associated NDI, although the benefit is reduced once chronic tubulointerstitial damage has occurred.
items.reference: HYPERNATREMIA: "Lithium causes NDI by multiple mechanisms, including direct inhibition of renal glycogen synthase kinase-3 (GSK3)... The entry of lithium through the amiloride-sensitive Na⁺ channel ENaC (Fig. 56-4) is required for the effect of the drug on principal cells, such that combined therapy within lithium and amiloride can mitigate lithium-associated NDI."

items.question: A 70-kg man has a plasma sodium of 160 mM. Using a total-body water estimate of 60% of body weight, what is his approximate free-water deficit?
items.explanation: TBW = 0.6 x 70 kg = 42 L. Free-water deficit = [(160-140)/140] x 42 L ≈ 6 L. This deficit should be replaced over 48-72 hours, limiting the fall in plasma Na+ to <10 mM/24h.
items.reference: TABLE 56-3 Management of Hypernatremia: "1. Estimate total-body water (TBW): 50% of body weight in women and 60% in men 2. Calculate free-water deficit: [Na⁺ - 140]/140 × TBW 3. Administer deficit over 48–72 h, without decrease in plasma Na⁺ concentration by >10 mM/24 h"

items.question: A woman at 36 weeks' gestation develops new-onset polyuria, polydipsia, and a plasma sodium of 148 mM. Which treatment is most appropriate?
items.explanation: Gestational DI results from accelerated degradation of native AVP by a placental 'vasopressinase' protease. DDAVP is resistant to this enzyme and remains effective, making it the treatment of choice; the condition resolves after delivery.
items.reference: HYPERNATREMIA: "gestational DI is a rare complication of late-term pregnancy wherein increased activity of a circulating placental protease with \"vasopressinase\" activity leads to reduced circulating AVP and polyuria, often accompanied by hypernatremia. DDAVP is an effective therapy for this syndrome, given its resistance to the vasopressinase enzyme."

items.question: A patient receiving intravenous insulin and dextrose for diabetic ketoacidosis has a serum potassium that drops from 4.5 to 3.0 mM within hours, without any increase in urinary or gastrointestinal potassium losses. What is the mechanism?
options: Redistribution of potassium from the extracellular fluid into cells
items.explanation: Insulin drives potassium into cells via stimulation of the Na+/K+-ATPase, a classic redistributive cause of hypokalemia that lowers serum K+ without any change in total-body K+ stores.
items.reference: HYPOKALEMIA: "Hypokalemia, defined as a plasma K⁺ concentration of <3.5 mM, occurs in up to 20% of hospitalized patients... Mechanistically, hypokalemia can be caused by redistribution of K⁺ between tissues and the ECF or by renal and nonrenal loss of K⁺."

items.question: A 24-year-old man of Asian descent with untreated hyperthyroidism develops sudden bilateral leg weakness at 3 a.m. after a large carbohydrate-rich dinner. Serum potassium is 2.1 mM. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
items.explanation: This presentation is classic for thyrotoxic periodic paralysis. Because the hypokalemia is redistributive (driven by catecholamine- and insulin-mediated Na+/K+-ATPase activation), high-dose propranolol (3 mg/kg) rapidly reverses the hypokalemia and paralysis and avoids the rebound hyperkalemia risk of aggressive K+ repletion.
items.reference: HYPOKALEMIA: "TPP develops more frequently in patients of Asian or Latin American origin... Patients with TPP typically present with weakness of the extremities and limb girdles, with paralytic episodes that occur most frequently between 1 and 6 a.m... high-dose propranolol (3 mg/kg) rapidly reverses the associated hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia, and paralysis."

items.question: A patient on a loop diuretic has persistent hypokalemia (K 3.0 mM) despite ongoing oral potassium chloride supplementation. Serum magnesium is 1.2 mg/dL. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Increase the potassium dose further without addressing magnesium
items.explanation: Magnesium depletion causes a secondary kaliuresis and increased distal nephron K+ secretion, making hypokalemia refractory to potassium replacement alone until magnesium is also corrected.
items.reference: HYPOKALEMIA: "Magnesium depletion has inhibitory effects on muscle Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase activity, reducing influx into muscle cells and causing a secondary kaliuresis. In addition, magnesium depletion causes exaggerated K⁺ secretion by the distal nephron... In consequence, hypomagnesemic patients are clinically refractory to K⁺ replacement in the absence of Mg²⁺ repletion."

items.question: A young woman presents with hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis. Her urine chloride is <10 mmol/L. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: A urine chloride <10 mmol/L in a hypokalemic, alkalotic patient points to surreptitious vomiting (e.g., bulimia), since volume depletion from vomiting drives avid renal Cl- retention — unlike Gitelman syndrome or diuretic abuse, where urinary Cl- is elevated.
items.reference: HYPOKALEMIA: "The most common causes of chronic hypokalemic alkalosis are surreptitious vomiting, diuretic abuse, and GS; these can be distinguished by the pattern of urinary electrolytes. Hypokalemic patients with vomiting due to bulimia will thus typically have a urinary Cl⁻<10 mmol/L; urine Na⁺, K⁺, and Cl⁻ are persistently elevated in GS... but less elevated in diuretic abuse and with greater variability."

items.question: A patient with a serum potassium of 2.6 mM and an estimated total-body deficit of ~600 mmol is being treated with intravenous potassium chloride. What is the most appropriate approach to replacement?
options: Replace the entire estimated deficit within 2-4 hours
Replace the deficit gradually over 24-48 hours with frequent monitoring of plasma potassium
Withhold all replacement until a magnesium level returns, regardless of the patient's symptoms
Replace only with oral potassium, regardless of severity
items.explanation: Because intracellular potassium stores re-equilibrate slowly, the deficit must be replaced gradually over 24-48 hours with frequent monitoring to avoid transient overrepletion and iatrogenic hyperkalemia.
items.reference: TREATMENT Hypokalemia: "In the absence of abnormal K⁺ redistribution, the total deficit correlates with serum K⁺, such that serum K⁺ drops by ~0.27 mM for every 100-mmol reduction in total-body stores; loss of 400–800 mmol of total-body K⁺ results in a reduction in serum K⁺ by ~2.0 mM. Notably, given the delay in redistributing potassium into intracellular compartments, this deficit must be replaced gradually over 24–48 h, with frequent monitoring of plasma K⁺ concentration to avoid transient overrepletion and transient hyperkalemia."

items.question: A patient with stage 4 chronic kidney disease (eGFR 22 mL/min) is found to have a serum potassium of 6.2 mM. What is the most likely underlying mechanism?
options: Redistribution due to insulin deficiency alone
items.explanation: Although redistribution and reduced cellular uptake can acutely raise serum K+, a decrease in renal K+ excretion — as expected with advanced CKD — is the most frequent underlying cause of hyperkalemia.
items.reference: HYPERKALEMIA: "Hyperkalemia is defined as a plasma potassium level of 5.5 mM, occurring in up to 10% of hospitalized patients; severe hyperkalemia (>6.0 mM) occurs in ~1%, with a significantly increased risk of mortality. Although redistribution and reduced tissue uptake can acutely cause hyperkalemia, a decrease in renal K⁺ excretion is the most frequent underlying cause."

items.question: A patient with heart failure on lisinopril and spironolactone develops a serum potassium of 6.0 mM. What is the most likely mechanism?
options: Combined inhibition of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis, reducing renal potassium excretion
items.explanation: ACE inhibitors and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists both reduce aldosterone activity. When prescribed together, their hyperkalemic effects compound, making this combination a predictable and common cause of hyperkalemia.
items.reference: HYPERKALEMIA: "Most medications associated with hyperkalemia cause inhibition of some component of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis. ACE inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, renin inhibitors, and MLRs are predictable and common causes of hyperkalemia, particularly when prescribed in combination."
